question: Rev 18:4 'come out of her my people.' God's people inside Babylon. OT come-out tradition (Abraham, Exodus, literal Babylon). 2 Cor 6:14-18. Hina-me purpose clause. John 10:16. Fall-then-call sequence.
prior_research_summary: Thirteen prior studies provide extensive foundation. 3am-10 established Babylon as apostate religious system using the zanah/porneia chain (H2181/G4202). 3am-11 identified the wine as false teaching creating participatory communion (1 Cor 10:16-21). 3am-12 demonstrated the doubled epesen epesen as prophetic aorist, the egeneto verb of transition showing progressive moral deterioration, and mapped twelve verbal parallels between Jer 50-51 and Rev 17-18. The prior rev-18-babylon-fall-come-out study covered the aorist imperative exelthate (G1831), the possessive ho laos mou, the synkoinoneo word (G4790), and the anti-Jubilee indictment. This study must go deeper on the come-out theology with fresh research, particularly the OT come-out chain, the hina-me purpose clause grammar, the John 10:16 fold/flock distinction, and the 2 Cor 6:14-18 composite citation.

required_verses.reason: Crux text — 'come out of her my people' with dual hina-me purpose clauses and sins reaching heaven
Expanded fall announcement immediately preceding the come-out call — egeneto verb, demon-dwelling imagery
Original fall announcement — doubled epesen, establishes the fall-then-call sequence
Repeated fall announcement with additional corruption detail — demon habitation language
Judgment specification following the come-out call — 'double unto her double' and 'in one hour' timing
Vindication of holy ones, apostles, prophets — God avenges them against Babylon
Blood of prophets and saints found in her — ultimate reason for the come-out call
Other sheep not of this fold — aule vs poimne distinction, God's flock transcends institutional folds
True shepherd enters by the door; sheep hear his voice — framework for the come-out call as shepherd's voice
My sheep hear my voice — the come-out call requires recognition of the shepherd's voice
NT composite citation of Isa 52:11 with heterozygeo, five antitheses, and chiastic OT catena
OT come-out command — 'depart ye, depart ye, go ye out from thence, touch no unclean thing'
OT come-out command — 'flee out of the midst of Babylon, deliver every man his soul'
Closest verbal parallel to Rev 18:4 — 'my people, go ye out of the midst of her'
OT come-out command — 'remove out of the midst of Babylon, go forth out of the land of the Chaldeans'
OT come-out command — 'go ye forth of Babylon, flee ye from the Chaldeans'
Post-exilic come-out call — 'flee from the land of the north' with covenant people still dwelling in Babylon
Abraham's original come-out call — 'get thee out of thy country' from Ur/Chaldea = proto-Babylon
Lot's come-out from Sodom — urgency, angels compelling departure, proximity = shared destruction
Korah rebellion — 'depart from the tents of these wicked men, touch nothing of theirs' lest shared destruction
Lo-ammi / ammi reversal — 'not my people' becomes 'my people' — illuminates who ho laos mou are
Reversal fulfilled — 'I will say to them which were not my people, Thou art my people'
Paul quotes Hosea reversal — applies to Gentile inclusion, people called from outside covenant boundaries
Peter quotes Hosea — 'which in time past were not a people, but are now the people of God'
First of three synkoinoneo/darkness parallels — 'have no fellowship with the unfruitful works of darkness'
Second synkoinoneo occurrence — positive use (sharing in distress), illuminates the verb's participatory meaning
Transfer language — 'delivered us from the power of darkness, translated us into the kingdom of his dear Son'
Two-cup framework — cup of the Lord vs cup of demons, table participation requiring departure
First angel's message — the gospel call that Babylon rejects, triggering the fall-then-call sequence
Source text for 2 Cor 6:16b — 'I will walk among you and be your God'
Parallel source for 2 Cor 6:16b — 'my tabernacle also shall be with them'
Source text for 2 Cor 6:18 — Father-child language altered from singular to plural
Background for heterozygeo (2 Cor 6:14) — 'thou shalt not let thy cattle gender with a diverse kind'
Sodom's cry reaching God — parallel to Rev 18:5 'sins have reached unto heaven'
Distinction between God's people and Egypt during plagues — type of come-out separation
Israel's exodus from Egypt — 'rise up, get you forth from among my people' — Pharaoh's forced come-out
OT source for 'Babylon is fallen, is fallen' — doubled form quoted in Rev 14:8 and 18:2
Seven last plagues — what the come-out call aims to deliver God's people from
Pouring out of plagues — 'go your ways and pour out the vials of the wrath of God upon the earth'
Conversion as come-out — 'ye turned to God from idols' — NT pattern of the OT come-out call
Laodicean self-deception — parallels blindness of those inside Babylon who do not recognize their condition
Gospel preached to Abraham — connects the come-out call to the everlasting gospel (3am-04)
Stephen recounts Abraham's call from Ur/Chaldea — confirms Gen 12:1 as come-out paradigm
Abraham obeyed by faith — 'he went out, not knowing whither he went'
Lot delivered from Sodom — 'just Lot, vexed with the filthy conversation of the wicked'
Faithful city become a harlot — template for Babylon's identity as fallen covenant community
God seeking his scattered sheep — shepherd seeking flock in every place where they have been scattered
Return, backsliding children — God calls people out with promise of shepherds who feed with knowledge
Lost sheep parable — shepherd seeks the one that has gone astray, parallels God seeking his people in Babylon
Harlot identification — mother of harlots and abominations, context for the system God's people must leave
Plague after Korah — proximity to rebellion brings destruction, reinforces Num 16:26 come-out pattern

focus_areas.title: The dual hina-me purpose clause in Rev 18:4
focus_areas.what: Analyze the grammatical structure of the two hina-me clauses and what they reveal about the timing and logic of the come-out call
focus_areas.why: The purpose clause is the grammatical heart of the command — it reveals that God's people have NOT YET fully participated in Babylon's sins and NOT YET received her plagues, establishing a narrow window of opportunity
focus_areas.how: Parse Rev 18:4 with greek_parser.py, READ grammar reference entry on hina-me + aorist subjunctive, compare the two clauses (synkoinonesete vs labete)
focus_areas.narrative: READ the grammar reference entry at `$GRAMMAR_REF/greek/purpose-clause-hina-me-aorist-subjunctive.md` — it explicitly covers the Rev 18:4 double clause. Parse Rev 18:4 with `greek_parser.py --verse 'Rev 18:4'` to confirm the dual hina-me structure. The first clause uses synkoinonesete (G4790, aorist subjunctive of synkoinoneo — 'lest you share in her sins') and the second uses labete (aorist subjunctive of lambano — 'lest you receive her plagues'). Meyer identifies 'an explicit antithesis between tais hamartiais and ton plegon' — sharing sins leads to sharing plagues. The aorist subjunctive in both clauses views the action as a single potential event, not ongoing — they haven't done it yet, but could. Also read the `mood-aorist-imperative` grammar reference for the exelthate command form. The Bratcher & Hatton UBS Handbook confirms the dual purpose: (1) moral separation and (2) judgment avoidance. Cross-reference Rev 15:1 and 16:1 for the plagues (plegon) that the second clause references.

focus_areas.title: The OT come-out tradition: vocabulary and pattern chain
focus_areas.what: Trace the yatsa/exerchomai vocabulary bridge and the theological pattern from Abraham through the eschatological call
focus_areas.why: Rev 18:4 is not an isolated command but the climax of a tradition spanning the entire Bible — understanding the pattern reveals the theology behind the command
focus_areas.how: Retrieve and compare all OT come-out passages, run Hebrew parser on key texts, trace the yatsa (H3318) root through the chain
focus_areas.narrative: READ the word study at `$WORD_STUDIES/H3318-yatsa.md` for the full semantic range of yatsa. The OT come-out chain runs: Gen 12:1 (Abraham from Ur/Chaldea — 'get thee out'), Gen 19:12-17 (Lot from Sodom — angels compelling departure), Exo 12:31-33 (Israel from Egypt — 'rise up, get you forth'), Num 16:26 (departure from Korah — 'depart from the tents'), Isa 48:20 ('go ye forth of Babylon'), Isa 52:11 ('depart ye, depart ye'), Jer 50:8 ('remove out of the midst of Babylon'), Jer 51:6 ('flee out of the midst'), Jer 51:45 ('my people, go ye out of the midst of her'), Zech 2:6-7 ('flee from the land of the north'). Run `hebrew_parser.py` on Isa 52:11 and Jer 51:45 to trace the Hebrew verbs. The LXX of Isa 52:11 uses exerchomai (G1831), the same root as exelthate in Rev 18:4. Retrieve full chapter context for Jer 51 and Isa 52 to see the come-out commands in their prophetic setting. Note the pattern: each come-out call is preceded by an announcement of judgment and followed by judgment execution — the same pattern Rev 14:8 → 18:4 → 18:8 follows.

focus_areas.title: Ho laos mou — God's people inside Babylon: identity and ecclesiology
focus_areas.what: Determine who 'my people' (ho laos mou) are and how God can claim covenant people inside an apostate system
focus_areas.why: The possessive pronoun 'my' is theologically explosive — God claims these people as his own despite their institutional location
focus_areas.how: Study laos (G2992) and am (H5971) usage, trace the Hosea reversal pattern, compare with John 10:16 fold/flock
focus_areas.narrative: READ the word studies at `$WORD_STUDIES/G2992-laós.md` and `$WORD_STUDIES/H5971-am.md` for the full semantic range of 'people' in both Testaments. The phrase ho laos mou is a covenant designation — God calls them HIS people before they leave Babylon, not after. Parse Rev 18:4 to confirm the grammatical structure of the vocative address. Trace the Hosea reversal: Hos 1:9 (lo-ammi, 'not my people'), Hos 2:23 ('I will say to them which were not my people, Thou art my people'), Rom 9:25-26 (Paul applies to Gentile inclusion), 1 Pet 2:9-10 (Peter applies to the new covenant community). Does this pattern illuminate who the ho laos mou are in Rev 18:4? Gill distinguishes 'elect ones till now uncalled' from those 'secretly called but not publicly professing.' JFB affirms 'in every apostate or world-conforming church there are some of God's invisible and true Church.' Cross-reference with the ekklesia study at `$WORD_STUDIES/G1577-ekklesia.md` for the relationship between visible institution and invisible community.

focus_areas.title: John 10:16 — the aule/poimne distinction and God's flock beyond institutional folds
focus_areas.what: Analyze whether the Greek text supports the fold/flock distinction and what it means for God having people inside Babylon
focus_areas.why: If the KJV 'one fold' is a mistranslation of 'one flock' (poimne), the verse teaches that God's flock transcends institutional structures — directly applicable to Rev 18:4
focus_areas.how: Parse John 10:16 with greek_parser.py, study aule and poimne semantics, compare with Ezek 34
focus_areas.narrative: Parse John 10:16 with `greek_parser.py --verse 'John 10:16'` to confirm the aule/poimne distinction. READ the word study at `$WORD_STUDIES/G4166-poimen.md` for shepherd semantics. Web research unanimously confirms the KJV 'one fold' mistranslates poimne (flock) — Ellicott says 'It is not unity of fold which is regarded as the future of the Church, but unity of flock.' Bengel notes 'He says other sheep, not another sheep-fold.' The asyndetic construction 'mia poimne, heis poimen' (one flock, one shepherd) emphasizes identity without requiring institutional merger. The dei (G1163, divine necessity) in 'them also I MUST bring' indicates a divine mandate — READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G1163-dei.md`. Compare with Ezek 34:11-16 where God himself seeks scattered sheep. The fold/flock distinction explains how God can have ho laos mou in Babylon — they belong to his flock (poimne) even though they are in the wrong fold (aule). Run `cross_testament_parallels_v2.py --hybrid-nt 'John 10:16'` to find OT connections.

focus_areas.title: 2 Corinthians 6:14-18 — the composite OT catena and NT application of the come-out tradition
focus_areas.what: Map the chiastic structure of the OT citations and determine how Paul reapplies the come-out tradition through temple theology
focus_areas.why: This is the primary NT application of the OT come-out tradition outside Revelation — the composite citation reveals the theological logic of separation
focus_areas.how: Parse 2 Cor 6:14-18, verify the OT source texts, trace the heterozygeo etymology, analyze the chiastic structure
focus_areas.narrative: Parse 2 Cor 6:14-18 with `greek_parser.py --verse '2 Cor 6:14'` through 6:18. Lee Magness identifies a chiastically arranged catena: A (v.16b) Lev 26:12/Ezek 37:27 — divine dwelling and covenant formula; B (v.17a) Isa 52:11 — 'come out and be separate'; B' (v.17b) 'quit touching the unclean'; A' (v.18) 2 Sam 7:14 — Father-child covenant language (altered: singular to plural, 'son' to 'sons and daughters'). The opening word heterozygeo (2 Cor 6:14) is a NT hapax — READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G2087-heteros.md` for the heteros root. The verb derives from LXX Lev 19:19 — Paul frames the entire separation argument as a species-mixing violation. Verify each OT citation by retrieving the source texts. The five antitheses (righteousness/unrighteousness, light/darkness, Christ/Belial, believer/infidel, temple/idols) establish that the issue is ontological incompatibility, not mere preference. The closing formula 'says the Lord Almighty' (pantokrator) occurs only here and in Revelation in the NT — investigate the link.

focus_areas.title: The fall-then-call sequence: Rev 14:8 → Rev 18:2 → Rev 18:4
focus_areas.what: Analyze the textual and logical relationship between the fall announcement and the come-out call — does the text require the fall to be recognized before separation can occur?
focus_areas.why: The sequence determines the timing and mechanism of the come-out call — it flows from the announcement rather than existing independently
focus_areas.how: Compare Rev 14:8 and 18:2 expansions, trace the egeneto verb, analyze the sequential placement
focus_areas.narrative: Retrieve Rev 14:6-8 and Rev 18:1-5 with full chapter context. The sequence is: first angel's message (14:6-7, gospel call) → second angel's announcement (14:8, Babylon is fallen) → fourth angel's expanded announcement (18:1-3, with egeneto showing progressive deterioration and demon-dwelling imagery added) → come-out call (18:4, with hina-me purpose clauses) → judgment execution (18:8, 'in one hour'). The fall announcement makes the come-out call both POSSIBLE (people now understand the system's true character) and NECESSARY (judgment is imminent). READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G4098-pipto.md` for the pipto/epesen analysis already established in 3am-12. The egeneto (G1096) at 18:2 uses a verb of becoming/transition — READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G1096-ginomai.md`. Bohr claims Babylon falls BECAUSE she rejected the first angel's message — verify from the hoti clause in Rev 14:8 and the sequential placement. The time gap between 14:8 and 18:4 allows for progressive deterioration before the urgent come-out call.

focus_areas.title: Synkoinoneo (G4790) — the three-occurrence cluster and active co-participation
focus_areas.what: Study all three NT occurrences of synkoinoneo to determine whether it implies active co-participation rather than mere proximity
focus_areas.why: The verb choice in Rev 18:4 specifies WHAT makes staying in Babylon dangerous — not just location but shared participation in sin
focus_areas.how: Parse all three occurrences, compare semantic ranges, study the syn- prefix compound
focus_areas.narrative: Synkoinoneo (G4790) appears only three times in the NT: Eph 5:11 ('have no fellowship with the unfruitful works of darkness'), Phil 4:14 ('ye did communicate with my affliction' — positive sharing), and Rev 18:4 ('that ye be not partakers of her sins'). The syn- prefix intensifies koinoneo (G2841) — READ the library entry at `$WORD_STUDIES/G2841-koinoneo.md`. Parse all three verses with `greek_parser.py`. Bill Mounce notes the verb means 'to share in company with, be connected with, be a joint partaker.' The compound implies not mere awareness or proximity but active co-participation. Eph 5:11 provides the closest parallel — 'unfruitful works of darkness' maps to Babylon's sins. Phil 4:14 shows the same verb can describe positive sharing, confirming it means real participation in the referenced experience. Also check the related noun synkoinonos (G4791, 'co-participant') — it appears in Rom 11:17, 1 Cor 9:23, Phil 1:7, Rev 1:9. The koinonia word family (G2842) is the broader framework — retrieve the context from 1 Cor 10:16-21 where two tables are set and participation at one excludes the other.

focus_areas.title: The Sodom-Korah-Egypt triad: OT types of judgment-separation
focus_areas.what: Analyze the three OT come-out types (Lot from Sodom, Israel from Korah, Israel from Egypt) to determine the shared pattern of judgment-proximity separation
focus_areas.why: These are the three most vivid OT demonstrations of the principle that proximity to the judged means sharing their judgment — the very logic of Rev 18:4
focus_areas.how: Retrieve and compare Gen 19:12-17, Num 16:26, Exo 8:22-23; trace the shared vocabulary and pattern
focus_areas.narrative: The triad establishes a consistent pattern: (1) Gen 19:12-17 — Lot is commanded to leave Sodom before fire falls. 2 Pet 2:6-7 interprets this: God 'delivered just Lot, vexed with the filthy conversation of the wicked.' The urgency is physical — 'Escape for thy life; look not behind thee.' (2) Num 16:26 — Moses commands Israel to 'depart from the tents of these wicked men, and touch nothing of theirs, lest ye be consumed in all their sins.' The verb is sur (H5493) — depart, turn away. The warning is explicitly about shared destruction through proximity. Num 16:41-50 shows that even after the separation, a plague killed 14,700 of those who had complained. (3) Exo 8:22-23 — God puts a 'division' (peduth, H6304, distinction/separation) between his people and the Egyptians during the plagues. READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G4127-plege.md` for the plague vocabulary that connects the Egyptian plagues to Rev 15-16. The GotQuestions source notes: 'In times of judgment, God separates His people from those being judged.' The come-out call of Rev 18:4 is the eschatological version of this pattern.

focus_areas.title: The speaker identity and the voice from heaven
focus_areas.what: Determine who speaks in Rev 18:4 — is 'another voice from heaven' God, Christ, or an angelic messenger using covenant language?
focus_areas.why: The identity of the speaker determines the authority level of the command and whether 'my people' is a direct divine possessive
focus_areas.how: Parse the phone (voice) vocabulary, compare with other 'voice from heaven' instances in Revelation, check grammar of possessive
focus_areas.narrative: Rev 18:4 introduces the speaker as 'allon phonen ek tou ouranou' — another voice from heaven. READ `$WORD_STUDIES/G5456-phone.md` for the voice vocabulary in Revelation. The 'allon' (another, of the same kind) distinguishes this voice from the angel of 18:1 but still locates it in heaven. The possessive 'ho laos MOU' (MY people) is a covenant designation — in the OT, only God uses this phrase in the possessive first person. Bratcher & Hatton note: 'The words my people clearly identify the speaker as God himself (cross-ref 16:1).' But in Revelation, angelic messengers regularly speak on God's behalf. Parse Rev 18:4 to check whether the grammar clarifies the speaker. Compare with Rev 21:3 where 'a great voice out of heaven' uses similar covenant language. The John 10:16 connection is important — if the shepherd's voice calls the sheep, and the come-out call is the shepherd's voice, then the speaker is Christ calling his scattered flock. Read the `nominative-for-vocative` grammar reference at `$GRAMMAR_REF/greek/nominative-for-vocative.md` — this may be relevant if ho laos mou uses nominative for vocative address.

focus_areas.title: Sins reaching unto heaven — the Gen 18:20-21 / Rev 18:5 parallel
focus_areas.what: Analyze the verbal and conceptual parallel between Sodom's cry reaching God and Babylon's sins reaching heaven
focus_areas.why: The 'sins reaching heaven' language establishes the judicial threshold — the point at which judgment becomes inevitable and the come-out call becomes urgent
focus_areas.how: Compare Gen 18:20-21 with Rev 18:5, parse the Greek kollaō verb, trace the reaching-heaven motif
focus_areas.narrative: Rev 18:5 uses ekollethesan (from kollao, G2853, to glue/join) — her sins are 'glued together reaching unto heaven.' This parallels Gen 18:20-21 where the 'cry of Sodom and Gomorrah' reaches God, triggering the judgment sequence. Parse Rev 18:5 with `greek_parser.py --verse 'Rev 18:5'`. The verb kollao means to cement or join — the image is sins piled on sins, bonded together into a structure reaching heaven. This echoes the Tower of Babel (Gen 11:4 — a structure reaching heaven in rebellion), creating an inclusio with the original Babylon narrative. Also compare Ezra 9:6 ('our iniquities are increased over our head, and our trespass is grown up unto the heavens') and Jer 51:9 ('her judgment reacheth unto heaven'). The reaching-heaven motif establishes a judicial point of no return: once this threshold is crossed, judgment is inevitable and God's people must be removed. The mnemoneuo at 18:5 ('God hath remembered her iniquities') echoes Rev 16:19 ('great Babylon came in remembrance before God').

word_studies.description: exerchomai (go out, come out), G1831 — root of the exelthate command
laos (people), G2992 — ho laos mou covenant designation
yatsa (go out), H3318 — Hebrew come-out vocabulary
porneia (fornication), G4202 — Babylon's defining sin
pipto (fall), G4098 — epesen in the fall announcement
ginomai (become), G1096 — egeneto verb of transition in Rev 18:2
phone (voice), G5456 — voice from heaven in Rev 18:4
koinoneo (share/participate), G2841 — base verb for synkoinoneo
am (people), H5971 — OT 'my people' (ammi) designation
akathartos (unclean), G169 — demon habitation and separation command
hamartia (sin), G266 — sins of Babylon reaching heaven
plege (plague/blow), G4127 — plagues that the come-out call prevents
heteros (another of a different kind), G2087 — root of heterozygeo
poimen (shepherd), G4166 — one shepherd over one flock
dei (it is necessary), G1163 — divine necessity in John 10:16
kaleo (call), G2564 — God calling his people
zanah (commit fornication), H2181 — harlot vocabulary established in 3am-10
balal (confuse/mix), H1101 — Babel etymology established in 3am-10
pharmakeia (sorcery), G5331 — cognitive poison in Babylon's wine (3am-11)
bdelygma (abomination), G946 — what fills Babylon's cup (3am-11)

metanoeo (repent), G3340 — response required after the come-out call
poieo (do/make), G4160 — 'do' in context of Babylon's deeds

external_corpus_leads.directive: Verify by parsing both verses to check whether the imperative form matches

external_corpus_leads.claim: 2 Cor 6:16-18 employs a chiastically arranged catena of four OT citations (Lev 26:12, Isa 52:11, Ezek 37:27, 2 Sam 7:14)
external_corpus_leads.directive: Retrieve all four source texts and verify the citation structure in the Greek

external_corpus_leads.claim: The KJV 'one fold' in John 10:16 mistranslates poimne (flock) — the fold/flock distinction is load-bearing
external_corpus_leads.directive: Parse John 10:16 to confirm aule and poimne are different Greek words with different meanings

research_instructions:
This study is the FINAL study of Arc 2 (The Second Angel: Babylon Exposed). It must demonstrate how the come-out call is the pastoral/practical APPLICATION of the second angel's message — not just an announcement of Babylon's fall but a merciful intervention to rescue God's people before judgment falls. The research agent should:

1. Verify the EXACT verbal parallels between Rev 18:4 and the OT come-out passages (especially Jer 51:45 and Isa 52:11 LXX) by parsing both Greek and Hebrew.

2. Map the complete 2 Cor 6:14-18 catena — verify each OT citation and how Paul modifies the originals (especially the 'sons and daughters' addition to 2 Sam 7:14).

3. Confirm the aule/poimne distinction in John 10:16 by parsing the Greek — this is load-bearing for the ecclesiology argument about God's people in wrong institutional structures.

4. Trace synkoinoneo through all 3 NT occurrences with full context — the verb choice specifies WHAT makes remaining in Babylon dangerous (active co-participation, not mere proximity).

5. Check whether the Hosea lo-ammi/ammi reversal illuminates the identity of 'my people' in Rev 18:4 — are these people who were formerly 'not my people' and are being reclaimed?

6. The study should CONNECT all four studies in Arc 2: Babylon's identity (3am-10) → Babylon's wine (3am-11) → Babylon's fall (3am-12) → Come out of her (3am-13). The come-out call is the CONSEQUENCE of all three preceding findings — the system is identified, its deceptions exposed, its fall announced, and now God's people must leave.

7. Do NOT treat the come-out call as merely physical/institutional separation. The 2 Cor 6:14-18 passage and the synkoinoneo word study show it is about breaking PARTICIPATORY COMMUNION — the same communion established by Babylon's wine (3am-11) through the two-cup framework (1 Cor 10:16-21).

8. For the fall-then-call sequence, verify whether the text requires the fall to be RECOGNIZED before the come-out call can function — this has implications for the timing and mechanism of the call.
